video_title: Gita Gopinath on Why Interest Rates Have Surged All Around the World | Odd Lots
gist: Harvard economist and former IMF first deputy MD Gita Gopinath explains why real and nominal rates have risen globally—the AI capex boom, persistent ~7% US fiscal deficits, a more volatile marginal buyer of debt, the end of the savings glut and secular stagnation—and warns that high public debt plus an expectation of 'big lasting state support' (the BLISS trade) is increasingly unsustainable.

themes.theme: Why global rates have structurally risen

insights.claim: US R-star has drifted up from roughly half a percentage point pre-pandemic to about one percentage point (or higher), driven by higher expected inflation, large fiscal deficits, and AI capital expenditure demand.
insights.speaker: Gita Gopinath
insights.so_what: A structurally higher neutral rate implies ~3% nominal rates as the new floor (1% R-star + 2% inflation target), a regime shift from the low-for-long era.
insights.quote: The R star has drifted up from pre pandemic when it was like half a percentage point. Now it's a one percentage point.

insights.claim: Three things changed post-pandemic to push rates up: an unexpected AI investment boom raising private capital demand, US fiscal deficits projected to stay near 7% indefinitely, and a shift in the marginal debt buyer from central banks to volatile hedge funds and non-bank financials.
insights.speaker: Gita Gopinath
insights.so_what: The marginal-buyer shift means yield-curve volatility is now structurally higher; expect more sensitivity to global market conditions than in the QE era.

insights.claim: The global savings glut is over and secular stagnation has ended—proven by rising real rates—because the AI boom has solved the prior shortfall of private-sector investment.
insights.speaker: Gita Gopinath
insights.so_what: Investors should stop anchoring to the pre-2020 low-rate framework; capital is now scarce relative to demand, not glutted.
insights.quote: No, we don't have a global savings glut anymore. And proof of that is real rates going up.

insights.claim: The only major source of capital supply offsetting a very long list of demand sources (AI, fiscal, defense, energy security, reshoring) is savings from aging demographics worldwide.
insights.speaker: Gita Gopinath
insights.so_what: Demographic savings are the main thing keeping rates from going even higher; a counterintuitive reason aging populations suppress rates rather than raise them.
insights.quote: the reason interest rates are not much more high than they would have been is because of the supply of savings coming from aging demographics

themes.theme: AI crowding out and its two channels

insights.claim: AI now accounts for ~50% of all investment-grade corporate bond issuance year-to-date and nearly 40% of junk-rated debt, a scale Gopinath ties to genuine crowding-out pressure on sovereign bonds.
insights.speaker: Tracy Alloway / Gita Gopinath (citing Torsten Slok)
insights.so_what: Massive AI debt issuance competes directly with Treasuries for capital; investors substituting mega-cap IG debt for Treasuries can push sovereign yields higher.

insights.claim: Gopinath distinguishes two crowding-out channels: a financial one (AI capital demand raises real rates even with no inflation effect) and a real-economy one (AI competes for turbines, trucking, contractors, labor), and judges the real-rate channel currently more important than the inflation channel.
insights.speaker: Gita Gopinath
insights.so_what: Clarifies that today's higher rates are more a real-rate (capital demand) story than an AI-driven inflation story; current inflation pressure comes mainly from energy.

insights.claim: Companies' FOMO about being six months behind on AI models means higher rates do not slow AI capex the way they would in a normal cycle, because firms will finance data centers regardless to avoid being 'consigned to the permanent underclass.'
insights.speaker: Joe Weisenthal (citing Torsten Slok) / Gita Gopinath
insights.so_what: AI capex is rate-insensitive, weakening monetary policy's braking effect and supporting the case for higher-for-longer rates.
insights.quote: if the alternative is being consigned to the permanent underclass when the other company builds the most advanced model, you're going to do it nonetheless

themes.theme: Policy implications: good vs bad R-star

insights.claim: Whether a higher R-star is benign depends on its source: productivity-driven investment demand is the 'good' kind that lifts growth and lowers debt/GDP, while fiscal-deficit-driven borrowing is the 'bad' kind that raises R-star without the growth that should accompany it.
insights.speaker: Gita Gopinath
insights.so_what: Policymakers must diagnose the driver before reacting; a fiscally-driven R-star increase is more dangerous and harder to grow out of.

insights.claim: Regardless of the R-star source, with a 2% inflation target the US is looking at roughly 3% nominal rates as the baseline—a clear shift from the pre-pandemic regime—and the open question is whether an AI productivity boom lets the Fed avoid keeping rates above neutral.
insights.speaker: Gita Gopinath
insights.so_what: Sets a ~3% nominal-rate anchor for forward planning; whether the Fed must go higher hinges on AI productivity vs energy/immigration/trade-driven inflation.

insights.claim: If the Strait of Hormuz conflict is not resolved soon, crude could spike toward $160/barrel, triggering severe demand destruction and a scenario where the short end of rates gets cut rapidly.
insights.speaker: Gita Gopinath
insights.so_what: An oil-shock tail scenario could flip the front end of the curve dovish via demand destruction even amid higher long-end rates.

themes.theme: Public debt, the BLISS trade, and crisis risk

insights.claim: Gopinath views high public debt levels everywhere as the most worrisome shock in the world, and warns developed economies are moving into a space where borrowing costs are far more volatile and market-sensitive, as seen in the UK, France and even Japan.
insights.speaker: Gita Gopinath
insights.so_what: Developed-market sovereign debt is no longer a safe, low-volatility asset class; rate spikes can ripple into other asset classes and trigger credit crunches.
insights.quote: the one that's most worrisome is what we see with public debt levels everywhere

insights.claim: A debt crisis in an advanced economy that borrows in its own currency looks like a credit crunch leading to a financial crisis—sharp borrowing-cost spikes, an investment slump, and a debt-overhang that slows growth and dynamism—rather than a reserve-depletion default.
insights.speaker: Gita Gopinath
insights.so_what: Reframes advanced-economy debt risk: watch for credit-crunch/financial-crisis dynamics and rollover stress, not currency reserve depletion.

insights.claim: The 'BLISS' (Big Lasting State Support) trade—markets and policymakers assuming the state will always backstop shocks by protecting household spending and corporate profitability—is increasingly unsustainable given high debt, pushing governments toward unorthodox tools like price controls and financial repression.
insights.speaker: Gita Gopinath
insights.so_what: Investors pricing in a permanent state backstop may be mistaken; the next crisis may bring financial repression and price controls rather than fiscal rescue.
insights.quote: big lasting state support which has helped economies all over the world

insights.claim: Historically, countries reduce debt/GDP via a growth spurt, inflation, or default/restructuring; the developed world is now betting on AI lifting growth from ~2% to ~4%, and if that productivity payoff fails to materialize, debt pricing could deteriorate sharply.
insights.speaker: Gita Gopinath
insights.so_what: The entire developed-world fiscal outlook is wagered on AI delivering a productivity boom; a failed AI payoff is the key catalyst for a real debt crisis.
insights.quote: I believe that's what we're betting on this time with the eye. The hope is that with AI, we will get growth from 2% up to 4%.

insights.claim: If a central bank explicitly announced it would buy long-term debt to cap yields (outside a zero-lower-bound stimulus context), inflation expectations and real rates would rise and central-bank independence would end—so the strategy doesn't durably work.
insights.speaker: Gita Gopinath
insights.so_what: Yield-curve-capping as a debt-management tool is self-defeating; countries that try it (typically IMF clients) get a brief reprieve then much higher rates.

themes.theme: AI productivity uncertainty and the disinflationary boom

insights.claim: There is no evidence yet of the productivity wave (some analysts project +2 percentage points/year) that the AI bet relies on; Gopinath finds the technology terrific for her own productivity but notes it isn't affecting wages and the macro evidence is absent.
insights.speaker: Gita Gopinath
insights.so_what: A sober check on AI-productivity optimism; the gap between AI's market pricing and demonstrated macro productivity is a key risk to the whole rate/debt edifice.
insights.quote: There is no evidence right now of that kind of productivity wave coming through.

insights.claim: A 'disinflationary/deflationary boom' (powerful AI delivering material gains while costs of commodities and labor collapse) is theoretically possible, but Gopinath says no economist will assign meaningful probability to that benign scenario given the high uncertainty.
insights.speaker: Gita Gopinath
insights.so_what: The utopian AI-abundance scenario is conceivable but not a basis for positioning; uncertainty and under-discussed risk factors dominate.
insights.quote: I do not know a single person who will put a probability on that scenario and say that that's going to happen... with a significant amount of certainty

insights.claim: Foreign holdings of US equities have reached a historic ~$40 trillion, roughly twice the share of rest-of-world GDP seen just before the 2000 dot-com peak, making the world more invested in US equity than ever.
insights.speaker: Gita Gopinath
insights.so_what: Record global concentration in US equities is a systemic fragility; 'it's the only game in town' but leaves the world exposed if the AI trade disappoints.
insights.quote: the world has never been that invested in U.S. equity markets. It's like it's the only game in town.

insights.claim: The world has decisively shifted from an efficiency-based 'buy from the cheapest source' model to one where every country duplicates capital investment for security (energy, defense, semiconductors, rare earths) because of collapsed trust among trading partners.
insights.speaker: Gita Gopinath
insights.so_what: Capital-investment duplication across nations is structurally inflationary and capital-demanding, reinforcing the higher-rates regime.
insights.quote: we moved squarely, firmly, decisively away from the pure efficiency based model

insights.claim: Gopinath rejects the 'China makes everything and runs perpetual surpluses' narrative: China's current account surplus (~3% of GDP vs 10% pre-GFC) reflects consumption suppression and declining investment (2025 was the first year investment actually fell), not unstoppable manufacturing dominance.
insights.speaker: Gita Gopinath
insights.so_what: China's surplus signals domestic weakness (property crash, overcapacity), not strength; and trading partners will impose tariffs on Chinese export dumping, making the dominance narrative unsustainable.

insights.claim: Joe Weisenthal flags that big index funds will for the first time have to buy giant companies at their peak valuations without ever capturing the run-up gains, a historic moment for indexing and ETFs given the wave of mega-IPOs.
insights.speaker: Joe Weisenthal
insights.so_what: Passive vehicles forced to buy mega-caps at peaks embed real investment judgment into 'mirror-the-market' indices, a structural risk for index investors.
